questions.stem: A force of $10\ \text{N}$ is applied perpendicular to a wrench at a distance of $0.5\ \text{m}$ from the pivot. What is the magnitude of the torque about the pivot?
questions.explanation: For a force perpendicular to the lever arm, the torque is $\tau = rF = 0.5 \times 10 = 5\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$. The trap is $20\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$, which divides the force by the distance instead of multiplying, $10/0.5$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A force of $20\ \text{N}$ acts at a point $0.5\ \text{m}$ from a pivot, with the force making an angle of $30^\circ$ to the line joining the pivot to the point. What is the magnitude of the torque?
questions.explanation: Only the component of force perpendicular to the lever arm produces torque: $\tau = rF\sin\theta = 0.5 \times 20 \times \sin 30^\circ = 10 \times 0.5 = 5\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$. The trap is $8.7\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$, which uses $\cos 30^\circ$ and so takes the component along the arm, which exerts no torque.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A point mass of $2\ \text{kg}$ is fixed at the end of a light rod and rotates in a circle of radius $3\ \text{m}$ about the other end. What is its moment of inertia about the axis of rotation?
questions.explanation: For a point mass, $I = mr^2 = 2 \times 3^2 = 2 \times 9 = 18\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The radius is squared. The trap is $6\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which computes $mr$ and forgets to square the radius.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid uniform disk of mass $4\ \text{kg}$ and radius $0.5\ \text{m}$ rotates about its central axis. What is its moment of inertia? (For a solid disk, $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$.)
questions.explanation: Substituting into $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(0.5)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(0.25) = 0.5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The trap is $1\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which forgets the factor of one half and computes $MR^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid uniform sphere of mass $5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $0.2\ \text{m}$ rotates about a diameter. What is its moment of inertia? (For a solid sphere, $I = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2$.)
questions.explanation: Substituting, $I = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2 = \tfrac{2}{5}(5)(0.2)^2 = 2 \times 0.04 = 0.08\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The trap is $0.2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which uses $MR^2 = 5(0.04) = 0.2$ and drops the $\tfrac{2}{5}$ factor specific to a solid sphere.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform thin rod of mass $3\ \text{kg}$ and length $2\ \text{m}$ rotates about an axis through its centre, perpendicular to the rod. What is its moment of inertia? (Use $I = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2$.)
questions.explanation: Substituting, $I = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2 = \tfrac{1}{12}(3)(2^2) = \tfrac{1}{12}(3)(4) = \tfrac{12}{12} = 1\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The trap is $4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which uses the end-axis formula $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2 = \tfrac{1}{3}(12) = 4$ instead of the centre-axis one.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform thin rod of mass $3\ \text{kg}$ and length $2\ \text{m}$ has moment of inertia $1\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ about its centre. Using the parallel axis theorem, what is its moment of inertia about an axis through one end, perpendicular to the rod?
questions.explanation: The parallel axis theorem gives $I = I_{cm} + Md^2$, where $d = L/2 = 1\ \text{m}$ is the shift from the centre to the end. So $I = 1 + 3(1)^2 = 1 + 3 = 4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, matching the direct formula $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$. The trap is $1\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which forgets to add the $Md^2$ term and just reports the central moment of inertia unchanged.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid disk of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $0.5\ \text{m}$ has central moment of inertia $\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2 = 0.25\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. What is its moment of inertia about an axis on its rim, parallel to the central axis?
questions.explanation: By the parallel axis theorem with $d = R = 0.5\ \text{m}$, $I = I_{cm} + MR^2 = 0.25 + 2(0.5)^2 = 0.25 + 0.5 = 0.75\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which is $\tfrac{3}{2}MR^2$. The trap is $0.5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which adds $MR^2$ but mistakenly drops the original $I_{cm}$, or equivalently reports only the shift term.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A torque of $10\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$ acts on a flywheel of moment of inertia $2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. What is the resulting angular acceleration?
questions.explanation: The rotational analogue of Newton's second law is $\tau = I\alpha$, so $\alpha = \tau/I = 10/2 = 5\ \text{rad/s}^2$. The trap is $20\ \text{rad/s}^2$, which multiplies torque by moment of inertia instead of dividing.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A wheel with moment of inertia $4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ spins at $3\ \text{rad/s}$. What is its rotational kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Rotational kinetic energy is $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(3^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(9) = 18\ \text{J}$. The trap is $36\ \text{J}$, which omits the factor of one half and computes $I\omega^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A rotating body has moment of inertia $2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ and angular velocity $5\ \text{rad/s}$. What is the magnitude of its angular momentum?
questions.explanation: Angular momentum is $L = I\omega = 2 \times 5 = 10\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. The trap is $25\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, which uses $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(25) = 25$, confusing angular momentum with rotational kinetic energy.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A $0.5\ \text{kg}$ particle moves in a circle of radius $2\ \text{m}$ at a speed of $4\ \text{m/s}$. What is its angular momentum about the centre?
questions.explanation: For a particle moving perpendicular to the radius, $L = mvr = 0.5 \times 4 \times 2 = 4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. The trap is $2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, which uses $mv$ and forgets the radius, or otherwise drops a factor.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A spinning skater pulls her arms in, reducing her moment of inertia from $4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ to $2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. Her initial angular velocity is $3\ \text{rad/s}$. With no external torque, what is her new angular velocity?
questions.explanation: With no external torque, angular momentum is conserved: $I_1\omega_1 = I_2\omega_2$, so $\omega_2 = I_1\omega_1/I_2 = (4 \times 3)/2 = 12/2 = 6\ \text{rad/s}$. Pulling in halves the inertia and so doubles the spin rate. The trap is $1.5\ \text{rad/s}$, which divides the wrong way, halving the angular velocity as if inertia and spin moved together.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid cylinder rolls without slipping with its centre moving at speed $v$. What is the relationship between its angular velocity $\omega$ and $v$, where $R$ is its radius?
questions.explanation: Rolling without slipping means the contact point is instantaneously at rest, which requires $v = \omega R$, so $\omega = v/R$. The trap is $\omega = vR$, which multiplies instead of dividing and would give the wrong units (it must be $\text{m/s}$ divided by $\text{m}$ to yield $\text{rad/s}$).
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid cylinder of mass $m$ rolls without slipping. What fraction of its total kinetic energy is rotational? (For a solid cylinder, $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mR^2$.)
questions.explanation: The translational energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ and the rotational energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\tfrac{1}{2}mR^2)(v/R)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}mv^2$. The total is $\tfrac{3}{4}mv^2$, so the rotational fraction is $\tfrac{1/4}{3/4} = \tfrac{1}{3}$. The trap is $\tfrac{1}{2}$, which assumes the energy splits equally between translation and rotation.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid cylinder is released from rest and rolls without slipping down an incline, descending a vertical height of $1.5\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is the speed of its centre at the bottom? (For a solid cylinder, $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mR^2$.)
questions.explanation: Energy conservation gives $mgh = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \tfrac{3}{4}mv^2$ for a solid cylinder. So $v^2 = \tfrac{4}{3}gh = \tfrac{4}{3}(10)(1.5) = 20$, giving $v = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.47\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $\sqrt{30} \approx 5.48\ \text{m/s}$, which uses $v = \sqrt{2gh}$, the value for a frictionless slide that ignores the energy stored in rotation.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A disk of moment of inertia $0.25\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ experiences a net torque of $0.5\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$. Starting from rest, what is its angular velocity after $4\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: The angular acceleration is $\alpha = \tau/I = 0.5/0.25 = 2\ \text{rad/s}^2$. Starting from rest, $\omega = \alpha t = 2 \times 4 = 8\ \text{rad/s}$. The trap is $2\ \text{rad/s}$, which reports the angular acceleration $\alpha$ as if it were the final angular velocity and forgets to multiply by the time.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: Two solid spheres have the same mass but one has twice the radius of the other. What is the ratio of their moments of inertia (larger to smaller)?
questions.explanation: For a solid sphere $I = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2$, so at fixed mass $I$ scales as $R^2$. Doubling the radius multiplies the moment of inertia by $2^2 = 4$, giving a ratio of $4 : 1$. The trap is $2 : 1$, which assumes $I$ scales linearly with radius rather than with its square.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A hoop and a solid disk have the same mass and radius and are released from rest at the top of the same incline, both rolling without slipping. Which reaches the bottom first?
options: The hoop, because more of its mass is at the rim.
They arrive together, since mass and radius are equal.
The solid disk, because it has the smaller moment of inertia.
The hoop, because it has the smaller moment of inertia.
questions.explanation: For rolling down an incline, $a = g\sin\theta/(1 + I/MR^2)$. The disk has $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$ (so $1 + \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{3}{2}$) while the hoop has $I = MR^2$ (so $1 + 1 = 2$). The smaller denominator for the disk gives it the larger acceleration, so it wins. The trap is that they arrive together; the distribution of mass, through the moment of inertia, decides the race, not the total mass.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A grindstone in the shape of a solid disk has moment of inertia $0.5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ and spins at $20\ \text{rad/s}$. A constant braking torque of $2\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$ is applied. How long does it take to stop?
questions.explanation: The angular deceleration is $\alpha = \tau/I = 2/0.5 = 4\ \text{rad/s}^2$. The time to bring $\omega = 20\ \text{rad/s}$ to zero is $t = \omega/\alpha = 20/4 = 5\ \text{s}$. The trap is $10\ \text{s}$, which divides the angular velocity by the torque directly, $20/2$, skipping the moment of inertia.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and length $1.5\ \text{m}$ is pivoted at one end and held horizontal, then released. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. What is its angular acceleration the instant it is released? (For a rod about its end, $I = \tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$.)
questions.explanation: Gravity acts at the centre, a distance $L/2 = 0.75\ \text{m}$ from the pivot, so the torque is $\tau = mg(L/2) = 2(10)(0.75) = 15\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$. The moment of inertia is $I = \tfrac{1}{3}(2)(1.5)^2 = \tfrac{1}{3}(2)(2.25) = 1.5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. So $\alpha = \tau/I = 15/1.5 = 10\ \text{rad/s}^2$. The trap is $20\ \text{rad/s}^2$, which uses the full length as the lever arm, $\tau = mgL = 30\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$, instead of the half-length to the centre of mass.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A flywheel stores rotational kinetic energy of $400\ \text{J}$ while spinning at $20\ \text{rad/s}$. What is its moment of inertia?
questions.explanation: From $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$, $I = 2KE/\omega^2 = 2(400)/400 = 800/400 = 2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The trap is $1\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which omits the factor of $2$ and computes $KE/\omega^2 = 400/400$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle of mass $3\ \text{kg}$ moves with velocity $4\ \text{m/s}$ along a straight line whose perpendicular distance from a fixed point $O$ is $2\ \text{m}$. What is the magnitude of its angular momentum about $O$?
questions.explanation: Angular momentum about a point is $L = mv d$, where $d$ is the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of motion: $L = 3 \times 4 \times 2 = 24\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Even a particle moving in a straight line has angular momentum about an off-line point. The trap is $12\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, which uses $mv$ and forgets the perpendicular distance.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A hoop (ring) of mass $2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $0.5\ \text{m}$ rotates about its central axis. What is its moment of inertia? (For a hoop, $I = MR^2$.)
questions.explanation: All the mass of a hoop sits at radius $R$, so $I = MR^2 = 2(0.5)^2 = 2(0.25) = 0.5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The trap is $0.25\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which uses $\tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$, the disk formula, even though a hoop has all its mass at the rim and so a larger moment of inertia.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: When a planet moves in an elliptical orbit, its angular momentum about the Sun stays constant. As it moves from far away (aphelion) to nearby (perihelion), what happens to its speed?
options: It speeds up, because angular momentum $mvr$ is conserved and $r$ decreases.
It slows down, because it is closer to the Sun.
It stays the same, because the orbit is closed.
It speeds up, because the Sun's gravity does no work.
questions.explanation: The gravitational force points along the line to the Sun, so it exerts no torque about the Sun and the angular momentum $L = mvr$ (for the perpendicular component) is conserved. As $r$ decreases toward perihelion, the speed $v$ must increase to keep $L$ fixed. This is Kepler's second law in disguise. The trap is that it slows down; being closer actually makes it move faster, exactly because $L$ is conserved.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid uniform disk of mass $4\ \text{kg}$ and radius $0.5\ \text{m}$ rotates about its central axis at $6\ \text{rad/s}$. Use $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$. Find the moment of inertia and the rotational kinetic energy.
questions.solution: The moment of inertia is $I = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(0.5^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4)(0.25) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared. The rotational kinetic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)(6^2) = $ {{2}} joules.
questions.explanation: The moment of inertia is $0.5\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, and the kinetic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}(0.5)(36) = 9\ \text{J}$. The factor of one half appears in both the disk formula and the kinetic energy.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform thin rod of mass $3\ \text{kg}$ and length $2\ \text{m}$ rotates about a perpendicular axis. Use $I_{cm} = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2$. Find the moment of inertia about the centre and about one end.
questions.solution: About the centre, $I_{cm} = \tfrac{1}{12}(3)(2^2) = $ {{1}} kilogram metres squared. By the parallel axis theorem with $d = 1\ \text{m}$, about the end $I = I_{cm} + Md^2 = 1 + 3 = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared.
questions.explanation: The centre value is $\tfrac{1}{12}(3)(4) = 1\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. Shifting to the end adds $Md^2 = 3(1)^2 = 3$, giving $4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, which matches $\tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$ directly.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A torque of $10\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$ acts on a flywheel of moment of inertia $2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, starting from rest. Find the angular acceleration and the angular velocity after $3\ \text{s}$.
questions.solution: The angular acceleration is $\alpha = \tau/I = 10/2 = $ {{1}} radians per second squared. After $3\ \text{s}$ from rest, the angular velocity is $\omega = \alpha t = $ {{2}} radians per second.
questions.explanation: The angular acceleration is $5\ \text{rad/s}^2$, and after $3\ \text{s}$ the angular velocity is $5 \times 3 = 15\ \text{rad/s}$. The rotational kinematics mirror the linear case with $\tau \to F$, $I \to m$, $\alpha \to a$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A skater spinning at $3\ \text{rad/s}$ with moment of inertia $4\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ pulls her arms in, reducing her moment of inertia to $2\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. Find her new angular velocity and state which quantity is conserved.
questions.solution: Conserving angular momentum, $\omega_2 = I_1\omega_1/I_2 = (4 \times 3)/2 = $ {{1}} radians per second. The quantity conserved here is the {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The new spin rate is $12/2 = 6\ \text{rad/s}$. Angular momentum $L = I\omega$ is conserved because no external torque acts; the rotational kinetic energy actually increases, supplied by the work the skater does pulling her arms in.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A solid cylinder rolls without slipping down an incline, descending a vertical height of $1.5\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and use $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mR^2$. Find the speed of its centre at the bottom and the fraction of its kinetic energy that is rotational.
questions.solution: Energy conservation gives $mgh = \tfrac{3}{4}mv^2$, so $v^2 = \tfrac{4}{3}(10)(1.5) = 20$ and $v = \sqrt{20} \approx $ {{1}} metres per second. Of the total kinetic energy, the rotational part is the fraction {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The speed is $\sqrt{20} \approx 4.47\ \text{m/s}$. The rotational energy $\tfrac{1}{4}mv^2$ out of the total $\tfrac{3}{4}mv^2$ is one third; the other two thirds are translational. A frictionless slide would instead give $v = \sqrt{2gh} \approx 5.48\ \text{m/s}$.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics

questions.stem: A force of $20\ \text{N}$ acts at a point $0.5\ \text{m}$ from a pivot. Find the torque when the force is perpendicular to the lever arm, and when it makes $30^\circ$ with the arm.
questions.solution: When perpendicular, the torque is $rF = 0.5 \times 20 = $ {{1}} newton metres. When the force makes $30^\circ$ with the arm, the torque is $rF\sin 30^\circ = 0.5 \times 20 \times 0.5 = $ {{2}} newton metres. The component of force that produces torque is the one {{3}} to the lever arm.
questions.explanation: Perpendicular gives the maximum torque $10\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$; at $30^\circ$ only $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5$ of the force is effective, halving it to $5\ \text{N}\,\text{m}$. Torque uses the component of force perpendicular to the lever arm.
questions.source: in the style of Kleppner and Kolenkow, An Introduction to Mechanics
